---
title: "Research Data Working Group (2022): Findings and Recommendations for OneIT"
author: "Reviewed by Tim Dennis, UCLA Library Data Science Center"
date: "March 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 10pt
    linestretch: 1.2
---

**Source:** *A Call to Action: A Roadmap for Expanding Research Infrastructure and Data Services at UCLA* (December 2, 2022). Commissioned by five executive sponsors: <abbr title="Vice Chancellor for Research">VCR</abbr>, University Librarian, <abbr title="Associate Vice Chancellor & Chief Information Officer">AVC & CIO</abbr>, Vice Provost for IT, and <abbr title="Chief Information Officer">CIO</abbr> of Health Sciences. 18-month effort; campus-wide engagement and peer benchmarking across 13 service categories.

---

## Key Findings That Remain Relevant Today

- **UCLA falls behind peers in 8 of 13 research data service categories.** The lowest score was for a cohesive research data services program with a website and service catalog — the most basic coordination infrastructure.
- **90% of UCLA's \$7M annual enterprise storage spend occurs at the departmental level.** This fragmentation produces redundancy, inconsistent security, and no economies of scale.
- **There is no central ownership of research data services.** Multiple units respond to campus-wide issues (funder mandates, storage crises, compliance requirements) independently and inconsistently. <abbr title="Principal Investigators">PIs</abbr> navigate this alone: *"death by a thousand cuts."*
- **UCLA's <abbr title="National Science Foundation">NSF</abbr> per-award average (\$307K) is 15–30% below UCSB (\$428K) and UCSD (\$412K).** The <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> identified missing infrastructure for large, multi-site proposals as a contributing factor.
- **Funder mandates have outpaced UCLA's capacity.** The <abbr title="National Institutes of Health">NIH</abbr> <abbr title="Data Management and Sharing Policy">DMSP</abbr> (effective January 2023) and the <abbr title="Office of Science and Technology Policy">OSTP</abbr> public access mandate (effective December 2025) require institutional infrastructure UCLA does not yet have in place.

---

## Relevant Recommendations for OneIT

The <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> recommended a **layered model** — not consolidation, but coordination. Central services raise the floor; local expertise is preserved where research happens. This maps directly onto what OneIT can address:

**1. Create a central research data coordination function.**
The <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> proposed an Office of the Research Data Rockstar (<abbr title="Office of the Research Data Rockstar">ORDR</abbr>) to consolidate ownership, accountability, and faculty support. Three years later this role still does not exist. OneIT's governance structure is the right place to establish it — with authority under the <abbr title="Vice Chancellor for Research">VCR</abbr>, not IT operations.

**2. Establish a shared research storage platform with baseline faculty allocation.**
The proposed Bruin Research Data Hub would provide every tenure-track faculty member a baseline storage allocation at no cost. This directly addresses the 90% departmental fragmentation finding and is the highest-leverage infrastructure investment the <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> identified.

**3. Centralize what scales; preserve what requires domain expertise.**
Networking, <abbr title="Identity and Access Management">IAM</abbr>/SSO, cybersecurity, cloud procurement, and shared storage are appropriate for central consolidation. Research computing, data services consulting, and domain-specific systems (<abbr title="High-Performance Computing">HPC</abbr>, secure enclaves, discipline-specific repositories) require embedded expertise and researcher proximity that does not survive enterprise IT consolidation. Peer institutions that have outpaced UCLA share this model.

**4. Build the P3/P4 compliant environment for restricted data.**
Export controls on AI and semiconductor research, clinical data requirements, and <abbr title="International Traffic in Arms Regulations">ITAR</abbr>-adjacent research are a growing share of UCLA's portfolio. This infrastructure was recommended in 2022 and is now more urgent.

---

## Evidence Supporting Research Enablement Needs

- **Peer benchmarking (13 categories):** Available in the <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> report and *Research on Data Services II.* Identifies specific gaps relative to Michigan, Stanford, UCSD, and others — most of which have invested in coordinated research data infrastructure since 2022.
- **<abbr title="National Science Foundation">NSF</abbr> and <abbr title="National Institutes of Health">NIH</abbr> award data:** UCLA's per-award gap and the scale of its <abbr title="National Institutes of Health">NIH</abbr> portfolio (\$505M annually) document both the opportunity and the compliance risk.
- **Researcher use cases:** Appendix 3 of the roadmap includes discipline-specific scenarios documenting where infrastructure gaps directly impede research.
- **Cost of inaction:** The Google Drive 10PB migration crisis (June 2024 deadline), estimated \$1.8M/year in potential overage fees, is a concrete recent example of what distributed, uncoordinated storage costs.
- **Phase 2 materials (2023–2024):** Implementation task analysis, Deans Council brief, and Bruin Data Hub specifications provide additional detail on feasibility and sequencing.

*All source documents available for download on the [Resources](resources.qmd) page.*

---

## What Has Changed Since 2022 — and What Persists

**What has changed:**

- **<abbr title="National Institutes of Health">NIH</abbr> <abbr title="Data Management and Sharing Policy">DMSP</abbr> took effect January 2023** — six weeks after the roadmap published. Compliance is now required, not anticipated.
- **<abbr title="Office of Science and Technology Policy">OSTP</abbr> public access mandate took effect December 2025** — all federally-funded research must be publicly available at no cost. This is present reality, not a future risk.
- **Generative AI** has created cross-disciplinary <abbr title="Graphics Processing Unit">GPU</abbr> compute demand the roadmap did not anticipate. Access to AI infrastructure is now a research equity issue on par with storage in 2022.
- **Federal funding uncertainty has intensified** (March 2026): UCLA's ability to compete for large, multi-site awards is more consequential than ever.
- **One IT launched**, raising new organizational questions about research IT roles that the <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> did not address.

**What no longer applies as written:**

- Cost figures ($74.2M / $49.8M) reflect 2022 market conditions; updated estimates would be needed.
- "Future" mandates (<abbr title="National Institutes of Health">NIH</abbr> <abbr title="Data Management and Sharing Policy">DMSP</abbr>, <abbr title="Office of Science and Technology Policy">OSTP</abbr>) are now in force — the urgency framing has shifted from anticipatory to reactive.

**What persists:**

- UCLA's benchmarking gaps remain: none of the four core recommendations have been implemented.
- The coordination vacuum (no <abbr title="Office of the Research Data Rockstar">ORDR</abbr> equivalent) is still unaddressed.
- Faculty reliance on informal workarounds — servers under desks, fragmented storage, ad hoc cloud spend — continues to be documented in recent campus discussions. It matches exactly what the <abbr title="Research Data Working Group">RDWG</abbr> found in 2022.
- **The <abbr title="Geographic Information Systems">GIS</abbr> librarian vacancy is a concrete local example of the mechanism in action.** A December 2025 report by <abbr title="Office of Advanced Research Computing">OARC</abbr>, Software Central, and the Library on ArcGIS Online governance documented the positive arc clearly: a <abbr title="Geographic Information Systems">GIS</abbr> specialist built out the platform, broadened access, and drove campus adoption — usage grew. When that position became vacant, the platform that researchers were actively depending on lost its designated administrator and governance structure. It now operates at 230-240% of licensed storage capacity with no retention policy and real risk of Esri service disruption. (Horne & Woltag, <abbr title="Office of Advanced Research Computing">OARC</abbr>/Software Central/Library, December 2025)
- The layered model remains the right architecture: **central coordination raises the floor; embedded expertise stays where research happens.**

```{=html}
<script>
(function() {
  var tip;
  document.querySelectorAll('abbr[title]').forEach(function(el) {
    el.addEventListener('mouseenter', function(e) {
      tip = document.createElement('div');
      tip.textContent = el.getAttribute('title');
      tip.style.cssText = 'position:fixed;background:#2d2d2d;color:#fff;padding:4px 10px;border-radius:4px;font-size:0.8em;line-height:1.4;z-index:9999;pointer-events:none;white-space:nowrap;box-shadow:0 2px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);';
      document.body.appendChild(tip);
      moveTip(e);
    });
    el.addEventListener('mousemove', moveTip);
    el.addEventListener('mouseleave', function() {
      if (tip) { tip.remove(); tip = null; }
    });
  });
  function moveTip(e) {
    if (!tip) return;
    var x = e.clientX + 12, y = e.clientY - 36;
    if (x + tip.offsetWidth > window.innerWidth - 8) x = e.clientX - tip.offsetWidth - 12;
    tip.style.left = x + 'px';
    tip.style.top = y + 'px';
  }
})();
</script>
```
